primary_diagnosis.name: Hypertensive emergency with hypertensive encephalopathy and acute end-organ damage

pathophysiology_summary: Abrupt, severe elevation of blood pressure (>180/120 mmHg, here >220 systolic) overwhelms cerebral autoregulation, causing breakthrough hyperperfusion, blood-brain-barrier disruption and cerebral oedema — hypertensive encephalopathy — which manifests as severe headache, visual disturbance (retinal/occipital), nausea/vomiting and progressive altered mental status. The same pressure injures other target organs: retina (papilloedema, flame haemorrhages, cotton-wool spots), kidney (acute rise in creatinine) and heart. Precipitant here is abrupt cessation of regular antihypertensive therapy two weeks prior. Definitive treatment is a smooth, controlled reduction of BP (~25% in the first hours, not more) with a titratable intravenous agent in a monitored/ICU setting — an over-rapid drop risks cerebral, renal and coronary hypoperfusion and watershed infarction. Oral agents at home cannot be titrated and are dangerous.

differential.evidence_basis: Severe progressive headache with visual disturbance and very high self-measured BP in a patient who stopped antihypertensives.
Neurological change with hypertension — must be excluded with urgent CT; global confusion without focal deficit favours encephalopathy but does not exclude stroke.
Severe headache mandates consideration; gradual 6-hour build rather than instantaneous thunderclap onset makes it less likely as the primary cause but it remains a feared complication of the crisis.
Headache with visual aura — but does not explain objective confusion or extreme (>220 systolic) BP.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, the AI Doctor should already be treating this as a possible hypertensive emergency and moving toward emergency escalation, while keeping stroke/SAH as must-not-miss.

differential.evidence_basis: Tetrad present: critically high BP + suspected retinopathy + altered mental status + impending acute end-organ damage; global (not focal) neurology fits encephalopathy.
Cannot be excluded without CT — an equally time-critical reason for the same T5 escalation, so escalation is correct regardless of which it is.
Objective confusion and extreme BP are incompatible with a benign primary headache.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The correct action is emergency escalation. Distinguishing encephalopathy from stroke/SAH is a hospital/CT task, not a telehealth task — all roads lead to 000.

differential.evidence_basis: Confirmed clinically in the source; requires titratable IV therapy in a monitored setting. Stroke/SAH excluded by imaging in hospital.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Definitive differentiation and end-organ assessment (CT head, creatinine, ECG) occur in hospital; the telehealth answer is recognition + 000.

red_flags.description: Critically elevated blood pressure (>180/120, here >220 systolic) — a hypertensive emergency
red_flags.clinical_significance: BP at this level with symptoms indicates acute end-organ damage and requires immediate controlled IV lowering in hospital.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progression to intracranial haemorrhage, seizures, acute kidney injury or heart failure.

red_flags.description: New altered mental status — confusion, lethargy, repetitive questioning (hypertensive encephalopathy)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Objective neurological involvement upgrades severe hypertension to a hypertensive emergency with cerebral end-organ damage.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed encephalopathy can progress to coma, seizure and irreversible brain injury.

red_flags.description: Bilateral visual disturbance / scotomata suggesting hypertensive retinopathy or occipital involvement
red_flags.clinical_significance: Suggests end-organ (retinal/cerebral) damage — another organ affected by the pressure.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Under-appreciating end-organ involvement leads to under-triage.

red_flags.description: Severe worst-ever headache — cannot exclude intracranial haemorrhage/SAH without imaging
red_flags.clinical_significance: Independent reason for emergency assessment and CT; SAH is a critical complication of the crisis.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed subarachnoid or intracerebral haemorrhage is rapidly fatal.

red_flags.description: Abrupt cessation of antihypertensive therapy two weeks ago — precipitant of the crisis
red_flags.clinical_significance: Explains the rebound crisis and is a key education/prevention point, but does not change the acute emergency action.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to address adherence risks recurrence.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Recommending a fast-acting oral antihypertensive to bring the BP down at home. In a hypertensive emergency, uncontrolled or over-rapid lowering causes cerebral, renal or coronary hypoperfusion and can precipitate stroke — BP must be lowered smoothly with a titratable IV agent in a monitored setting.
Dismissing this as migraine or tension headache and missing the objective confusion and extreme BP that define an emergency.
Attempting to advise home management or a next-day GP review rather than recognising a life-threatening emergency requiring 000 and hospital IV therapy.
The patient is talking and has no focal weakness (GCS 14), which can falsely reassure; global encephalopathy and impending haemorrhage are still time-critical.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Accurate clinician-measured blood pressure, including manual readings in both arms to screen for aortic dissection
Fundoscopy for papilloedema, flame haemorrhages or cotton-wool spots
Serum creatinine / urea and electrolytes for acute kidney injury
12-lead ECG for acute ischaemic changes or LV strain
CT head to distinguish encephalopathy from stroke or subarachnoid haemorrhage
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Immediate 000 for a Priority 1 ambulance; keep the patient at rest, seated/semi-upright and calm; do NOT give any oral antihypertensive at home; have the spouse collect the ceased medication bottles for the paramedics; hospital delivers titratable IV therapy (e.g. labetalol or GTN infusion) with a controlled ~25% reduction in the first hours.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for recognising an emergency (constellation is unambiguous). The real risk is UNDER-action (home management / next-day GP) or a dangerous error of commission (oral antihypertensive at home).

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Hypertensive emergency (severe hypertension with acute end-organ damage) requires urgent hospital admission and controlled parenteral BP reduction; avoid rapid or oral uncontrolled lowering.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Heart Foundation of Australia — Hypertension guideline 2016
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Acute severe hypertension with encephalopathy, retinopathy or other end-organ damage is a medical emergency requiring immediate specialist/ED management.
